questions.question:
Hit by floods, the kharif (summer sown) crops in various parts of the country have been affected. Officials believe that the loss in production of the kharif crops can be recovered in the output of the rabi (winter sown) crops so that the country can achieve its food-grain production target of 291 million tons in the crop year 2019-20 (July - June). They are hopeful that good rains in July-August will help the soil retain moisture for a longer period, helping winter sown crops such as wheat and pulses during the November-February period.

Which of the following statements can be inferred from the given passage?

A. Officials declared that the food-grain production target will be met due to good rains.
B. Officials want the food-grain production target to be met by the November-February period.
C. Officials feel that the food-grain production target cannot be met due to floods.
D. Officials hope that the food-grain production target will be met due to a good rabi produce.
questions.answer: D. Officials hope that the food-grain production target will be met due to a good rabi produce.
questions.to_find: Which statement can be correctly inferred from the passage about official hopes regarding the food-grain production target

understand.plain: The passage describes how flood damage to kharif (summer) crops may be offset by stronger rabi (winter) crops. Officials are hopeful, not declarative. The key inference must match the passage's tone exactly - officials believe and are hopeful, not certain. Option D correctly captures the inference: hope that rabi produce will meet the production target.

keywords.explain: Summer-sown crops (planted around June-July, harvested October-November) such as rice, sorghum, and cotton
keywords.example: Rice is a kharif crop that is affected when monsoon rains cause floods in July-August

keywords.explain: Winter-sown crops (planted around November, harvested March-April) such as wheat and pulses
keywords.example: Wheat is a rabi crop that benefits from moisture retained in soil after a good monsoon

keywords.explain: An inference drawn from a passage must match the tone and content precisely; 'hopeful' and 'believe' differ significantly from 'declared' or 'cannot be met'
keywords.example: Saying officials 'declared' something overstates certainty; the passage only says they 'believe' and are 'hopeful'

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a textual inference question about agriculture and official statements

given.aim: Identify the statement that can be correctly inferred from the passage

terms.meaning: Kharif production has been reduced due to floods in various parts of the country
terms.example: Excess rains that help moisture retention can also cause floods that damage standing summer crops
terms.connects: Creates a production deficit that officials hope rabi crops will cover

terms.meaning: Officials believe (not declare with certainty) that the loss can be recovered through rabi output
terms.example: Using 'believe' signals a projection based on expected conditions, not a guarantee
terms.connects: Eliminates option A which says 'declared' - too strong a word

terms.term: food-grain production target of 291 million tons
terms.meaning: The overall national target for crop year 2019-20 (July to June) that officials still aim to achieve
terms.example: Even if kharif output falls, the combined kharif + rabi total may still reach 291 million tons
terms.connects: This target is what officials hope rabi produce will allow them to meet

terms.meaning: Officials are optimistic that good July-August rains will retain soil moisture and boost wheat and pulses in Nov-Feb
terms.example: Moisture retained from monsoon helps winter crops germinate and grow without additional irrigation
terms.connects: Directly supports option D: hope that food-grain target will be met due to a good rabi produce

given.plan: Match the tone (hopeful/belief, not certainty) and the subject (rabi produce offsetting kharif loss) to the four options; eliminate options that overstate certainty (A), misstate timing (B), or contradict the passage (C)

steps.title: Identify the passage's main claim and tone
steps.apply: Officials BELIEVE the loss can be recovered via rabi crops and are HOPEFUL about good rains. These are tentative, optimistic statements - not declarations or certainties.
steps.note: Tone words like 'believe' and 'hopeful' constrain which options are valid

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: A says 'declared' - too strong, the passage uses 'believe'. B says target to be met 'by November-February' - the passage mentions November-February as the growing period, not the target deadline. C says 'cannot be met due to floods' - directly contradicts the passage which says the target CAN be achieved.
steps.note: Each wrong option misrepresents the passage in a specific, identifiable way

steps.title: Verify option D
steps.apply: D states officials HOPE the food-grain target will be met due to a GOOD RABI PRODUCE. This precisely matches the passage: officials are hopeful (correct tone), and they expect rabi output to recover the deficit (correct mechanism).
steps.note: D is the only option that faithfully reflects both the tone and content of the passage

solution.result: D. Officials hope that the food-grain production target will be met due to a good rabi produce.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question requires no mathematical formulas; inference is drawn by carefully matching the tone and content of the passage to the answer choices.

questions.question:
Repo rate is the rate at which Reserve Bank of India (RBI) lends commercial banks, and reverse repo rate is the rate at which (RBI) borrows money from commercial banks.

Which of the following statements can be inferred from the above passage?

A. Decrease in repo rate will increase cost of borrowing and decrease lending by commercial banks.
B. Increase in repo rate will decrease cost of borrowing and increase lending by commercial banks.
C. Increase in repo rate will decrease cost of borrowing and decrease lending by commercial banks.
D. Decrease in repo rate will decrease cost of borrowing and increase lending by commercial banks.
questions.answer: D. Decrease in repo rate will decrease cost of borrowing and increase lending by commercial banks.
questions.to_find: Which statement correctly captures the effect of a decrease in repo rate on commercial banks' borrowing cost and lending

understand.plain: Repo rate is what RBI charges commercial banks when they borrow from it. When the repo rate decreases, the cost for commercial banks to borrow from RBI falls. With cheaper funds, banks can lend more at lower rates to customers, increasing lending activity. This is the standard monetary policy transmission mechanism.

keywords.explain: The rate at which RBI (the central bank) lends money to commercial banks; a lower repo rate means cheaper borrowing for banks
keywords.example: If repo rate falls from 6% to 5%, banks can borrow from RBI at 1% less, reducing their funding cost

keywords.explain: The rate at which RBI borrows from commercial banks; when banks park surplus funds with RBI, they earn this rate
keywords.example: A bank with excess liquidity lends to RBI at the reverse repo rate, earning a safe return

keywords.explain: The interest rate that borrowers (including banks) pay when they take loans; directly tied to the repo rate for banks
keywords.example: If repo rate decreases, banks' cost of funds drops, so they can offer cheaper loans to customers

understand.visual_alt: No diagram required; this is a logic and inference question about interest rate mechanics

given.aim: Determine the correct effect of a change in repo rate on commercial bank behavior

terms.meaning: RBI lends to commercial banks at the repo rate
terms.example: If repo rate = 6%, banks pay 6% annual interest when they borrow from RBI
terms.connects: Repo rate directly sets the cost at which banks obtain funds from the central bank

terms.meaning: RBI reduces the interest rate charged to commercial banks for overnight borrowing
terms.example: RBI cuts repo rate from 5.5% to 5.0%; banks can now borrow Rs. 100 crore paying Rs. 5 crore instead of Rs. 5.5 crore per year
terms.connects: A lower repo rate reduces the cost of funds for banks, enabling cheaper loans to the public

terms.meaning: When banks' cost of funds falls, they can lend more aggressively at lower rates, increasing overall lending
terms.example: A bank that was reluctant to lend at 8% may now lend at 7% after its repo rate cost drops by 0.5%
terms.connects: Connects decrease in repo rate to increase in lending - confirming option D

terms.meaning: Decrease in repo rate -> decrease in cost of borrowing AND increase in lending (expansionary effect)
terms.example: Central banks cut repo rates during recessions to stimulate lending and economic activity
terms.connects: Matches option D exactly; options A, B, C all have at least one direction wrong

given.plan: Use the definition given in the passage to trace the logical chain: repo rate decrease -> cheaper for banks to borrow from RBI -> banks' cost of borrowing decreases -> banks lend more -> lending increases

steps.title: Establish the causal chain from the passage
steps.apply: Repo rate = rate at which RBI lends to commercial banks. If repo rate DECREASES, the cost for commercial banks to borrow from RBI goes DOWN. Lower funding cost means banks can offer loans at lower rates, so lending INCREASES.
steps.note: The passage itself gives the definition; the inference follows from basic economic logic

steps.title: Check each option against the causal chain
steps.apply: A: decrease repo -> INCREASE cost of borrowing (WRONG - cost falls). B: increase repo -> DECREASE cost (WRONG - higher repo means higher cost). C: increase repo -> decrease cost (WRONG - opposite direction). D: decrease repo -> decrease cost AND increase lending (CORRECT - matches the chain).
steps.note: Options A, B, C all reverse the direction of at least one effect

steps.title: Confirm option D
steps.apply: Decrease repo rate -> cost of borrowing for banks falls -> banks pass on savings -> lending increases. Option D correctly states: 'Decrease in repo rate will decrease cost of borrowing and increase lending by commercial banks.'
steps.note: This is the standard monetary policy transmission: rate cut -> cheaper credit -> more lending

solution.result: D. Decrease in repo rate will decrease cost of borrowing and increase lending by commercial banks.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question tests comprehension of cause-and-effect relationships in monetary policy terminology; no mathematical formulas are required.

questions.question:
The American psychologist Howard Gardner expounds that human intelligence can be subcategorized into multiple kinds, in such a way that individuals differ with respect to their relative competence in each kind. Based on this theory, modern educationists insist on prescribing multi-dimensional curriculum and evaluation parameters that enable development and assessment of multiple intelligences.

Which of the following statements can be inferred from the given text?

A. Howard Gardner insists that the teaching curriculum and evaluation needs to be multi-dimensional.
B. The author of the above is of the opinion that teaching curriculum and evaluation needs to be multi-dimensional.
C. Howard Gardner wants to develop and assess the theory of multiple intelligences.
D. Modern educationists insist that the teaching curriculum and evaluation needs to be multi-dimensional.
questions.answer: D. Modern educationists insist that the teaching curriculum and evaluation needs to be multi-dimensional.
questions.to_find: Which statement is correctly inferred from the passage regarding who insists on multi-dimensional curriculum and evaluation

understand.plain: The passage makes a clear distinction of roles: Gardner is the psychologist who proposes the theory of multiple intelligences. It is the MODERN EDUCATIONISTS (not Gardner) who, based on his theory, insist on multi-dimensional curriculum and evaluation. A careful reading assigns actions to the correct subjects.

keywords.explain: American psychologist who proposed the Theory of Multiple Intelligences, arguing humans possess distinct kinds of intelligence (linguistic, logical, musical, spatial, etc.)
keywords.example: Gardner's theory suggests a student weak in mathematical intelligence may excel in musical or interpersonal intelligence

keywords.explain: The idea that human intelligence is not a single general factor but a set of distinct cognitive abilities in which individuals vary
keywords.example: Under this theory, a child's artistic ability counts as a valid form of intelligence, not just academic scores

keywords.explain: Contemporary education experts and practitioners who apply psychological theories to teaching practices and curriculum design
keywords.example: Educationists may design exams with practical, creative, and analytical sections to assess different types of intelligence

understand.visual_alt: No diagram needed; this is a reading comprehension question about attribution of claims to the correct actors

given.aim: Identify who is attributed with the insistence on multi-dimensional curriculum and evaluation

terms.meaning: Gardner expounds (explains/proposes) the theory that intelligence is multi-dimensional
terms.example: Gardner formulates the theory but the passage does not say he personally insists on curriculum changes
terms.connects: Eliminates option A (attributes curriculum insistence to Gardner) and option C (misrepresents Gardner's goal)

terms.meaning: Based on Gardner's theory, modern educationists insist on multi-dimensional curriculum and evaluation
terms.example: Educationists are the practitioners who translate the psychological theory into educational policy and curriculum design
terms.connects: Directly supports option D as the correct inference

terms.meaning: The author of the passage reports what Gardner and educationists believe, but does not personally assert that curriculum should be multi-dimensional
terms.example: Reporting someone else's opinion differs from expressing one's own - the author uses third-person reporting language
terms.connects: Eliminates option B which incorrectly attributes the insistence to the author

terms.meaning: In inference questions, the subject who holds a view or performs an action must match what the passage states
terms.example: Passage says 'educationists insist' - any option attributing this to Gardner or the author is incorrect
terms.connects: Key reasoning step that leads to selecting D over A, B, and C

given.plan: Read carefully to match each claim in the options to who actually makes or holds that claim in the passage; the passage attributes the insistence on multi-dimensional curriculum specifically to 'modern educationists'

steps.title: Map actions to subjects in the passage
steps.apply: Gardner: EXPOUNDS that intelligence is multi-dimensional. Modern educationists: INSIST on multi-dimensional curriculum/evaluation based on Gardner's theory. The author: REPORTS these views without personally asserting them.
steps.note: Careful attribution prevents confusing Gardner's theoretical claim with educationists' practical insistence

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A: Says Gardner insists on curriculum change - WRONG, the passage says EDUCATIONISTS insist. B: Says the author insists - WRONG, the author only reports others' views. C: Says Gardner wants to develop and assess the theory - WRONG, Gardner expounds the theory; educationists assess multiple intelligences. D: Says modern educationists insist on multi-dimensional curriculum/evaluation - CORRECT, directly stated in the passage.
steps.note: Only D correctly identifies the subject (educationists) and the action (insist on multi-dimensional curriculum/evaluation)

steps.title: Confirm D as the correct inference
steps.apply: The passage literally states: 'modern educationists insist on prescribing multi-dimensional curriculum and evaluation parameters'. Option D restates this precisely without adding or distorting any information.
steps.note: A direct inference - the most reliable answer is the one closest to the passage's literal words

solution.result: D. Modern educationists insist that the teaching curriculum and evaluation needs to be multi-dimensional.

questions.formula_note: This verbal reasoning question tests careful attribution of claims to the correct actors in a passage; no mathematical formulas are required.

questions.question:
Nominal interest rate is defined as the amount paid by the borrower to the lender for using the borrowed amount for a specific period of time. Real interest rate calculated on the basis of actual value (inflation-adjusted), is approximately equal to the difference between nominal rate and expected rate of inflation in the economy.

Which of the following assertions is best supported by the above information?

A. Under high inflation, real interest rate is low and borrowers get benefited.
B. Under low inflation, real interest rate is high and borrowers get benefited.
C. Under high inflation, real interest rate is low and lenders get benefited.
D. Under low inflation, real interest rate is high and lenders get benefited.
questions.answer: A. Under high inflation, real interest rate is low and borrowers get benefited.
questions.to_find: Which assertion about the relationship between inflation, real interest rate, and who benefits is best supported by the passage

understand.plain: The passage gives the key formula: real interest rate is approximately equal to nominal rate minus expected inflation. When inflation is HIGH, the real interest rate is LOW (nominal minus a large inflation figure). A lower real interest rate benefits borrowers because they repay in money that has lost purchasing power - they effectively pay back less in real terms. Lenders lose out in this scenario.

keywords.explain: The stated or face-value rate of interest on a loan, not adjusted for inflation; what appears in the loan contract
keywords.example: A bank offering a 10% annual loan is quoting the nominal interest rate

keywords.explain: The inflation-adjusted interest rate; approximately equal to nominal rate minus expected inflation rate
keywords.example: If nominal rate = 10% and inflation = 8%, real rate is approximately 10% - 8% = 2%

keywords.explain: Real rate is approximately equal to nominal rate minus inflation rate; when inflation rises, real rate falls (for a fixed nominal rate)
keywords.example: High inflation of 9% with nominal 10% gives real rate of only 1%; borrower benefits as repayment is in cheapened money

understand.visual_alt: No diagram required; this is a numerical logic and economic reasoning question about interest rates

given.aim: Use the relationship real rate = nominal rate - inflation to determine who benefits under high inflation

terms.term: real rate = nominal rate - expected inflation
terms.meaning: The formula given in the passage that links all three quantities
terms.example: Nominal = 8%, inflation = 6% -> real rate = 2%; nominal = 8%, inflation = 1% -> real rate = 7%
terms.connects: Core relationship needed to evaluate all four options

terms.term: high inflation effect on real rate
terms.meaning: When inflation is high, subtracting a large number from nominal rate results in a low (or negative) real rate
terms.example: If nominal rate is fixed at 7% and inflation jumps to 9%, real rate = 7% - 9% = -2% (real rate is low/negative)
terms.connects: Shows that high inflation -> low real rate, confirming first part of option A

terms.term: who benefits when real rate is low
terms.meaning: Borrowers benefit when real interest rates are low because they repay debt in money that has lost purchasing power relative to when they borrowed
terms.example: If you borrowed Rs. 1 lakh at 7% nominal but inflation is 8%, you repay less than you borrowed in real (purchasing power) terms - you gain
terms.connects: Confirms the second part of option A: borrowers get benefited under high inflation

terms.meaning: Low inflation means real rate remains high (nominal minus small inflation), which benefits lenders who receive repayment in money with retained purchasing power
terms.example: Nominal 7%, inflation 1% -> real rate 6%; the lender receives high real returns
terms.connects: Option D says low inflation -> high real rate -> lenders benefit; while correct in principle, the question asks for the assertion supported by the passage about the scenario in option A

given.plan: Apply the formula real rate = nominal rate - inflation to each scenario, then determine which party (borrower or lender) benefits from the resulting real rate

steps.title: Apply the formula to high inflation
steps.formula_raw: real rate = nominal rate - expected inflation
steps.apply: Under HIGH inflation: expected inflation is large -> nominal rate - large inflation = LOW real rate. Example: nominal = 8%, inflation = 7% -> real rate = 1% (low).
steps.note: This is the Fisher approximation stated directly in the passage

steps.title: Determine who benefits when real rate is low
steps.apply: When real interest rate is LOW, borrowers benefit. They repay debt with money that has depreciated in purchasing power. The real cost of their debt is lower than the nominal cost. Lenders, conversely, receive repayment in devalued money, so they lose out.
steps.note: This is a fundamental principle: inflation redistributes wealth from lenders to borrowers

steps.title: Evaluate all options and confirm A
steps.apply: A: High inflation -> low real rate -> borrowers benefit. CORRECT. B: Low inflation -> high real rate -> borrowers benefit. WRONG (high real rate hurts borrowers, helps lenders). C: High inflation -> low real rate -> lenders benefit. WRONG (lenders lose when real rate is low). D: Low inflation -> high real rate -> lenders benefit. TRUE in principle, but does not describe the assertion best supported by the passage's focus on borrowers vs lenders under high inflation. The passage is best matched by option A.
steps.note: Option A is the assertion most directly and completely supported by the given information

solution.result: A. Under high inflation, real interest rate is low and borrowers get benefited.

questions.formula_note: The Fisher approximation (real rate = nominal rate - inflation) is implicit in the passage definition; no formula registry entry is required as this is a verbal reasoning question, not a mathematics problem.
